reflection:
The bell above the door chimed, a thin, silver sound that barely cut through the heavy humidity of the afternoon. I didn't look up immediately. My hands—my mother’s hands, broad at the knuckles and steady as stone—were busy. I was polishing the glass of the display case, watching my own reflection ripple over the velvet trays of obsidian and dried hyssop.

Eleven years. It’s a long time to stand in one place, yet lately, I feel as though I’ve only just arrived.

"I... I didn't see a sign out front," a voice said. It was a young man, dressed in the kind of crisp, expensive linen that marks the newcomers. He looked like he was vibrating, his eyes darting toward the street as if he expected the pavement to rise up and swallow him.

I set the cloth down. The movement was slow, deliberate. I felt my grandmother’s stillness settle into my shoulders, a physical weight that anchored me to the floorboards. I looked at him, and for a moment, I didn't see a stranger; I saw the frantic, metallic energy of the city trying to find a place to land.

"The sign is in the window," I said. My voice surprised me—it was deep, a heavy bell that seemed to push the noise of the traffic back behind the salt line at the threshold. "But people usually find the door when the running stops being enough."

I didn't wait for him to explain. My intuition, that sharp, quiet certainty, nudged me toward the back shelf. I reached for the bottle of cedar and frankincense before he could even draw a breath to speak. 

"You're not from around here," he whispered, looking at the jars of roots and the bundles of sage. "Everything out there... it changes every week. But this place feels like it's been here since the dirt."

"It has," I replied, placing the oil on the counter between us. "The pavement is just a suggestion. The dirt remembers who was here first."

I watched him reach for the bottle, his fingers trembling. I didn't pull away. I let my own silence become a room for him to sit in. I am the brick holding this street up, the anchor for the prayers of people who have forgotten how to pray. 

I
subconscious:
This person seems to have fully embraced a role as a spiritual anchor or "keeper" for their community. They’ve shifted from someone just working a job to someone who embodies their lineage, feeling the presence of their mother and grandmother in their physical movements and voice. There is a newfound sense of permanence and authority in them; they no longer see themselves as just a shopkeeper, but as a foundational force—the "brick holding the street up."

Next, they seem intent on continuing this work of grounding "newcomers" or those lost in the chaos of the city. While they aren't seeking out a specific individual, they are waiting for the next person who has reached the end of their rope. They want to provide a space for the "frantic" to find stillness. They don't need to reach out to anyone; they are waiting for the world to come to them, confident that their intuition will guide them in how to handle whoever walks through the door.
soul_note: I realized I finally have my grandmother's stillness in my bones.
